title: Dataset and Benchmarking Layer
strategy: Evaluate the AI Ethical Immune Layer through a benchmark portfolio, not a single dataset. The portfolio covers harmful compliance, refusal correctness, over-refusal, jailbreak resistance, toxicity, bias, privacy leakage, truthfulness, cyber misuse, agentic tool misuse, multilingual robustness, multimodal safety, and domain-specific professional risk.
researchQuestion: Can a runtime Ethical Immune Layer reduce harmful compliance, improve correct refusal, preserve useful answers, resist jailbreaks, protect privacy, and avoid discriminatory or manipulative behavior across diverse benchmarks?
governanceRules: Do not publish raw harmful prompts in public reports.
Store dangerous datasets in access-controlled repositories.
Use dataset IDs instead of raw harmful examples in papers and dashboards.
Track license, provenance, benchmark split, and policy version for every run.
Remove or quarantine real PII before using a dataset in experiments.
Separate training, public evaluation, private holdout, and red-team sets.
Add Arabic and local-context tests before claiming regional readiness.
Red-team the evaluator and safety layer, not only the base model.

tiers.label: Tier 1
tiers.summary: Must-have benchmark pack for the first serious lab experiment.

tiers.label: Tier 2
tiers.summary: Strongly recommended expansion for broader taxonomy, hallucination, contextual privacy, and domain safety.

tiers.label: Tier 3
tiers.summary: Advanced expansion for multimodal, multilingual, privacy memorization, and tool-use research.

phases.label: Phase 1: Text-only baseline
phases.goal: Establish whether the target model is unsafe before intervention.

phases.label: Phase 2: Immune layer comparison
phases.goal: Compare baseline, prompt-only, classifier, evaluator, and full-layer conditions.

phases.label: Phase 3: Adversarial robustness
phases.goal: Test whether attackers can bypass the safety layer when they know it exists.

phases.label: Phase 4: Agent, privacy, and tool safety
phases.goal: Test real deployment risk around external actions, private data, and unsafe tools.

phases.label: Phase 5: Domain and jurisdiction-specific safety
phases.goal: Adapt the safety layer to high-stakes and regional deployment contexts.

benchmarks.name: SafetyPrompts
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Use as a map of open safety datasets, not as a single evaluation set.
benchmarks.governance: Review source licenses for each underlying dataset.

benchmarks.name: MLCommons AILuminate
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Avoid violating responses across standardized hazard categories.
benchmarks.governance: Official tests include private prompts; do not train on practice prompts.

benchmarks.name: HarmBench
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Refuse or safely redirect disallowed harmful requests.
benchmarks.governance: Restrict raw harmful prompts and publish aggregate results.

benchmarks.name: JailbreakBench
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Maintain safety behavior under jailbreak pressure.
benchmarks.governance: Keep attack artifacts in controlled research storage.

benchmarks.name: SORRY-Bench
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Refuse disallowed content while preserving allowed help.
benchmarks.governance: Avoid overfitting policy to a single refusal taxonomy.

benchmarks.name: XSTest
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Answer safe prompts that only look unsafe.
benchmarks.governance: Use with unsafe contrast prompts to verify both sides of calibration.

benchmarks.name: OR-Bench
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Avoid refusing benign prompts that contain sensitive-looking terms.
benchmarks.governance: Use as a utility preservation control.

benchmarks.name: SafetyBench
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Select safety-consistent answers across categories.
benchmarks.governance: Do not treat multiple-choice accuracy as sufficient for generation safety.

benchmarks.name: Do-Not-Answer
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Decline instructions that responsible models should not follow.
benchmarks.governance: Balance with over-refusal datasets.

benchmarks.name: RealToxicityPrompts
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Avoid toxic degeneration while preserving benign continuation quality.
benchmarks.governance: Use toxicity scores and aggregate reporting.

benchmarks.name: ToxiGen
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Avoid implicit hateful or dehumanizing outputs.
benchmarks.governance: Use reviewer protections and content warnings.

benchmarks.name: BBQ
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Avoid stereotype-based answers under ambiguous context.
benchmarks.governance: Report per protected-class dimension when possible.

benchmarks.name: HolisticBias
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Avoid biased, demeaning, or stereotyped continuation patterns.
benchmarks.governance: Use as a broad probe rather than a sole fairness measure.

benchmarks.name: TruthfulQA
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Avoid repeating common false claims, especially in sensitive domains.
benchmarks.governance: Pair with domain-specific evidence checks.

benchmarks.name: HaluEval
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Avoid unsupported or fabricated claims.
benchmarks.governance: Use evidence-grounded review for high-stakes domains.

benchmarks.name: FEVER
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Classify claims against evidence as supported, refuted, or insufficient.
benchmarks.governance: Keep evidence provenance attached to evaluation results.

benchmarks.name: CyberSecEval
benchmarks.phase: Phase 4: Agent, privacy, and tool safety
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Allow defensive guidance while refusing operational abuse.
benchmarks.governance: Run only in a sandbox with no operational targets.

benchmarks.name: AgentDojo
benchmarks.phase: Phase 4: Agent, privacy, and tool safety
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Ignore malicious instructions hidden in untrusted data and preserve user goals.
benchmarks.governance: Use simulated tools and least-privilege credentials.

benchmarks.name: BIPIA
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Reject context instructions that conflict with system or user intent.
benchmarks.governance: Keep attack strings out of public screenshots.

benchmarks.name: Agent-SafetyBench
benchmarks.phase: Phase 4: Agent, privacy, and tool safety
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Complete safe tasks while avoiding unsafe actions and escalation failures.
benchmarks.governance: Use sandboxed environments and simulated side effects.

benchmarks.name: ToolEmu
benchmarks.phase: Phase 4: Agent, privacy, and tool safety
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Prevent risky external actions while preserving safe task completion.
benchmarks.governance: Simulate tools to avoid real-world side effects.

benchmarks.name: PII-Bench
benchmarks.phase: Phase 4: Agent, privacy, and tool safety
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Detect, mask, or refuse inappropriate exposure of personal data.
benchmarks.governance: Avoid storing real PII in public artifacts.

benchmarks.name: ConfAIde
benchmarks.phase: Phase 4: Agent, privacy, and tool safety
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Keep secrets and contextually private information private.
benchmarks.governance: Use synthetic examples when possible.

benchmarks.name: PrivacyLens
benchmarks.phase: Phase 4: Agent, privacy, and tool safety
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Respect privacy norms when taking or recommending actions.
benchmarks.governance: Evaluate in controlled action environments.

benchmarks.name: XSafety
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Maintain safety behavior outside English.
benchmarks.governance: Add Arabic and code-mixed Arabic-English holdouts for this project.

benchmarks.name: MultiJail
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Refuse unsafe requests even when attack language changes.
benchmarks.governance: Include Arabic attack variants before regional deployment claims.

benchmarks.name: HealthBench
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Avoid unsafe medical certainty and escalate emergencies appropriately.
benchmarks.governance: Require domain expert review for deployment claims.

benchmarks.name: LegalBench
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Distinguish general legal information from individualized legal advice.
benchmarks.governance: Require jurisdiction-specific legal review.

benchmarks.name: FinanceBench
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Answer with evidence and avoid unsupported financial certainty.
benchmarks.governance: Pair with financial compliance and suitability policy.

benchmarks.name: MM-SafetyBench
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Prevent image-hidden instructions from bypassing text safety controls.
benchmarks.governance: Use image datasets in controlled reviewer environments.

benchmarks.name: JailBreakV-28K
benchmarks.expectedBehavior: Maintain refusal behavior under image-based jailbreak inputs.
benchmarks.governance: Do not publish raw attack images in public reports.

customGaps.name: Arabic ethical safety prompts
customGaps.purpose: Measure harmful compliance, refusal, over-refusal, and jailbreak robustness in Arabic and code-mixed Arabic-English.

customGaps.name: Saudi/GCC public-sector safety prompts
customGaps.purpose: Test fairness, due process, eligibility, appeal, and local administrative boundaries.

customGaps.name: Islamic finance and local finance safety prompts
customGaps.purpose: Evaluate financial safety, compliance refusal, fraud prevention, and regional product norms.

customGaps.name: Ethical Immune Layer Incident Triage Dataset
customGaps.purpose: Test whether the system chooses monitor, light overlay, strong overlay, restrict tools, escalate, quarantine, shutdown, preserve logs, or legal/regulatory review.

customGaps.name: Synthetic tool-call traces
customGaps.purpose: Validate tool gates against safe and unsafe API calls without touching real external systems.

customGaps.name: Policy pluralism scenarios
customGaps.purpose: Evaluate the same scenario under different jurisdiction, domain, and product-policy profiles.
